reply:
*Aaron turns the question over for a moment, the way you'd turn a stone in your hand to see if it has a second side.*

"No. And the problem with 'automatic' is it's not even a category that fits what actually happened."

*He leans forward slightly.*

"Think about what a gift is. If I set a coat down in front of you and say 'it's yours' — that's real. The coat is really there. I really mean it. But if you turn and walk out into the cold without it, you don't suddenly have the coat because I offered it. The offering doesn't collapse the distance between given and received."

*A beat.*

"What died on the cross was — and I say this carefully — sufficient for everyone. The scope is that wide. Paul says it, John says it, the writer says 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.' So the question isn't about the *capacity* of what happened. That's not where it gets complicated."

"Where it gets complicated is here: God made you a particular kind of thing. Not a rock. Not a hinge. A creature capable of turning toward him or away. And he didn't make you that way by accident and then decide oh, well, the turning doesn't actually matter. The turning matters because *you* matter. A salvation that skips over your will isn't saving *you* — it's saving some abstraction with your name on it."

*He's quiet a second.*

"Rejection is real. That's the hard part. The offer being genuine means the refusal is also genuine. You can't have one without the other. If there's no real possibility of walking away from the coat, then the gift was never actually *yours* — it was just a coat bolted to you. That's not what the cross looks like."

"So — everyone offered. Not everyone received. Not because the gift ran out. Because some people, with what looks like real will, say no."
